chunk_summary: Guidance on egg elimination in children with IgE-mediated allergy, ensuring protein and iron continuity through dietary replacements, label counseling, and reintroduction strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand the nutritional risks of egg elimination in children with IgE-mediated food allergy.
Identify appropriate protein and iron-rich replacements for egg in vegetarian and non-vegetarian diets.
Learn strategies for label reading and cross-contact precautions in Indian markets.
Recognize the role of supervised oral food challenges (OFC) in reintroducing baked egg.
key_facts: Egg elimination can lower dietary protein quality and micronutrient intake, including iron and vitamin B12.
Vegetarian replacements include pulses (dal, chana, rajma), soy products, and nut/seed pastes, while non-vegetarian options include meat, fish, or chicken.
Iron status should be monitored in vegetarian children, underweight children, or those with multiple eliminations; consider hemoglobin and ferritin testing if growth falters.
Egg is a common hidden ingredient in bakery products, mayonnaise, noodles, desserts, and snacks, requiring structured label reading.
Many children tolerate baked egg earlier than lightly cooked forms, but home trials are unsafe after systemic reactions; supervised OFC is recommended.
reference_quote: Egg contributes iron and vitamin B12; in children who are vegetarian, already underweight, or have multiple eliminations, review iron-rich foods and consider checking hemoglobin and ferritin if intake is low or growth is faltering. (Source: Dietary Guidelines for Indians, National Institute of Nutrition)
